revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Haji clan]][[ja:土師氏]][[zh:土師氏]]
{{Short description|Japanese clan}}
{{Infobox clan | clan name = Haji clan | native_name = | image badge = | image_size = | alt = | chiefs crest = | badge_caption = | chiefs motto = | chiefs slogan = | war cry = | country = Japan | region type = | region = | district type = | district = | ancestry = | ethnicity = | founder = | plant badge = | animal = | pipe music = | chiefs name = | image arms = | image_arms_size = | image_arms_alt = | chiefs title = | seat = | historic seat = | septs = | branches = | Allied clans = | Rival clans = | kindreds = | titles =
| last chiefs name = | date of death of last chief = | commander = }} The '''Haji clan''' ({{lang|ja|土師氏}}, ''Haji-uji, Haji-shi'') is a Japanese [[Uji (clan)|clan]]. The clan administered {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|qid=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware|1=Earthenware}} artisans, organized collectively into a group called ''Haji-be'' ({{lang|ja|土師部}}). During the {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato period|1=Yamato period}}, these artisans worked chiefly on soil-related matters, such as creating {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}, constructing {{ill|Tomb|en|Tomb|qid=Q381885|lt=Tomb|1=Tomb}}s and [[kofun]], and handling other civil engineering.<ref name="kotobank">[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%9C%9F%E5%B8%AB%E6%B0%8F-787168#E6.97.A5.E6.9C.AC.E5.A4.A7.E7.99.BE.E7.A7.91.E5.85.A8.E6.9B.B8.28.E3.83.8B.E3.83.83.E3.83.9D.E3.83.8B.E3.82.AB.29 Kotobank entry for ''Hajishi''] (in Japanese; retrieved 13 July 2022)</ref> The ''[[kabane]]'' titles of the clan are [[Sukune]] or [[Muraji]]. They were engaged in constructing the tombs of high-Ranking people as well as managing the funeral ceremonies of the [[okimi|''ōkimi'']] ("great lords").<ref name="kotobank" />
== History == The Haji clan claims descent from [[Ame no Hohi]], the second son of [[Amaterasu]], which makes them relatives of the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Family|Japanese Imperial Family]].<ref>{{cite book | last1=Cali | first1=J. | last2=Dougill | first2=J. | title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion | publisher=University of Hawaii Press | year=2012 | isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 | url=https://books.google.com/books?id=J1gEEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA69 | access-date=2020-11-21 | page=69}}</ref> [[Nomi no Sukune]] was believed to be the ancestor of the clan. According to legend, he was the inventor of ''{{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|qid=Q849376|lt=Haniwa|1=Haniwa}}'',<ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%9C%9F%E5%B8%AB%E9%83%A8-114219 Kotobank entry for ''Hajibe''] (in Japanese; retrieved 13 July 2022)</ref> the {{ill|Terracotta|en|Terracotta|qid=Q60424|lt=Terracotta|1=Terracotta}} clay figurines buried with a nobleman and used as a symbolic substitute for ''{{ill|Junshi|en|Junshi|qid=Q1071761|lt=Junshi|1=Junshi}}'', the practice whereby members of high-Ranking households would commit suicide upon the passing of the household head, as a way to continue serving them in death.
The clan was later divided into three houses: the [[Sugawara clan]], the {{ill|Akishino clan|ja|秋篠氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akishino clan|1=Akishino clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65277285}}, and the {{ill|Ōe clan|ja|大江氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōe clan|qq=}}.<ref name="kotobank" />
They are relatives of the [[Izumo clan]] who are also descended from [[Ame no Hohi]] and run [[Izumo-taishakyo]] today.<ref>{{Citation |title=Converting Japan, 1825–1875 |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.5040/9781474271110.ch-004 |work=The Origin of Modern Shinto in Japan : The Vanquished Gods of Izumo |page=38 |access-date=2023-10-24 |publisher=Bloomsbury Academic |isbn=978-1-4742-7108-0}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last=Cali |first=Joseph |url=https://www.amazon.ca/Shinto-Shrines-Sacred-Ancient-Religion/dp/0824837134 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012-11-30 |publisher=Latitude 20 |isbn=978-0-8248-3713-6 |edition=Illustrated |location=Honolulu |language=English}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Kuni no miyatsuko |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Matsunaga |first=Naomichi |title=Izumo kokusō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20231025020717/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8603 |archive-date=2023-10-25 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=Kokugakuin University Encyclopedia of Shinto |language=en}}</ref>
== Name == The clan takes its name from ''haji'' ({{lang|ja|[[:wikt:土師#Japanese|土師]]}}), a shift from older ''hani-shi'', from {{lang|ja|[[:wikt:埴#Japanese|埴]]}} (''hani'', "red clay", such as used to make {{ill|Terracotta|en|Terracotta|qid=Q60424|lt=Terracotta|1=Terracotta}}) + {{lang|ja|[[:wikt:師#Japanese|師]]}} (''-shi'', a Chinese-derived suffix appended to indicate "master" of a craft). The ''hani-shi'' were masters of the crafts of {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|qid=Q1377111|lt=Earthenware|1=Earthenware}} and {{ill|Earthworks (engineering)|en|Earthworks (engineering)|qid=Q11880367|lt=earthwork|1=Earthworks (engineering)}} engineering.
== See also == * [[Nomi no Sukune]] * [[Sugawara clan]] * {{ill|Hajinosato Station|en|Hajinosato Station|qid=Q5640000|lt=Hajinosato Station|1=Hajinosato Station}}
